attributes.stem:
A newborn boy is reviewed on the neonatal ward due to multiple physical abnormalities noted at birth. He was born at term via normal vaginal delivery to a 38-year-old woman. The pregnancy was uncomplicated, and prenatal screening was declined. He has no significant family history. He does not take any medications.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 160/min, blood pressure 65/40 mmHg, respiratory rate 60/min, SpO2 88% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. Physical examination reveals microcephaly, microphthalmia, cleft lip and palate, polydactyly, and a hypoplastic nose.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation and physical features described in this newborn are highly suggestive of Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome). Trisomy 13 is characterized by multiple congenital anomalies, including craniofacial defects, neurological abnormalities, and cardiac defects. The key features observed in this case that point towards Trisomy 13 include microcephaly, microphthalmia (abnormally small eyes), cleft lip and palate, polydactyly (extra fingers or toes), and a hypoplastic nose.

Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome) typically presents with characteristic facial features including upslanting palpebral fissures, epicanthal folds, and a flat nasal bridge. While intellectual disability is common, the severe congenital anomalies seen in this case are not typical of Down syndrome.

Trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome) shares some features with Trisomy 13, such as developmental delay and congenital heart defects. However, Trisomy 18 is typically associated with a prominent occiput, micrognathia, clenched fists with overlapping fingers, and rocker-bottom feet, which are not described in this case.

Turner syndrome affects only females and is characterized by short stature, webbed neck, broad chest, and ovarian dysgenesis. The severe congenital anomalies seen in this male infant are not consistent with Turner syndrome.

Klinefelter syndrome affects males but typically does not present with obvious physical abnormalities at birth. Features of Klinefelter syndrome, such as tall stature, gynecomastia, and small testes, become apparent later in life.

It's important to note that while the clinical features strongly suggest Trisomy 13, a definitive diagnosis would require genetic testing, typically through karyotyping or chromosomal microarray. Trisomy 13 is associated with a poor prognosis, with many infants not surviving beyond the first few days or weeks of life due to severe neurological and cardiac defects. Management typically focuses on supportive care and addressing any immediate medical needs.
learningPoints: Trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome) is characterized by multiple congenital anomalies, including cleft lip/palate, microphthalmia, and polydactyly.
Clinical features of trisomy 13 often include severe neurological and cardiac defects, leading to a poor prognosis.
Definitive diagnosis of trisomy 13 requires genetic testing, but clinical features can strongly suggest the diagnosis.
references: Khan U, Hussain A, Usman M, ul Abiddin Z. An infant with Patau syndrome associated with congenital heart defects. Ann Med Surg. 2022;80:104100. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36045789/

attributes.name: Junior
attributes.description: Suitable for a junior, pre-clinical medical student
